era_context: Early Bronze Age III. The 5th Dynasty of Egypt (c. 2494–2345 BCE in some chronologies) continues its rule. In Mesopotamia, the Early Dynastic Period III (c. 2900–2334 BCE) sees the city-states of Sumer and Akkad in competition; Lagash and Umma are engaged in their long-running border disputes. The Indus Valley Civilisation (c. 2500–1900 BCE) is in its mature phase, with major urban centres at Harappa, Mohenjo-daro, and Dholavira. This is an era of established state structures, monumental architecture, and interconnected trade networks.
geographic_coverage_gaps: East Asia beyond the Shang region (limited records)

events.title: Continued reign of Djedkare Isesi or early reign of Unas (5th Dynasty Egypt)
events.description: The year 2840 BCE falls within the late 5th Dynasty of Egypt's Old Kingdom. Depending on which chronological system is used, this year may fall within the reign of Djedkare Isesi (c. 2854–2837 BCE) or the early reign of Unas (c. 2837–2797 BCE). Both rulers continued the tradition of monumental pyramid construction and maintained the administrative apparatus of the Old Kingdom, though power was increasingly decentralised to provincial governors. This period represents the twilight of the Old Kingdom's centralised authority.
key_figures: Djedkare Isesi or Unas (Pharaoh of Egypt)

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from Saqqara and Abydos

events.certainty_note: Egyptian chronology of the Old Kingdom is uncertain by ±50–100 years depending on the system used. The assignment of 2840 BCE to a specific pharaoh depends on which chronological framework is adopted. The reign lengths from king lists are more reliable than absolute dates.

disconfirming_evidence: The traditional narrative of the late Old Kingdom as a period of simple decline has been complicated by recent scholarship. Some evidence suggests that the decentralisation of power to nomarchs was a deliberate administrative strategy rather than a loss of control. The collapse of the Old Kingdom at the end of the 6th Dynasty (c. 2181 BCE) is now understood as a complex process involving climate change (the First Intermediate Period drought), administrative fragmentation, and possibly external pressures, rather than a simple failure of royal authority. No specific events are reliably attested to the year 2840 BCE itself.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 2840 BCE is extremely sparse. We possess king lists (compiled centuries after the fact), inscriptions in tombs and temples, and archaeological evidence from excavations, but no contemporary written records documenting events of this specific year. Egyptian chronology remains contested; different scholarly systems produce dates that differ by a century or more. The surviving sources are heavily biased toward royal and elite narratives; the vast majority of the population left no written record. The Pyramid Texts, inscribed in royal tombs, provide religious and administrative information but not year-by-year historical narratives.

graph_edges.note: The late 5th Dynasty's administrative decentralisation contributed to the eventual collapse of the Old Kingdom c. 2181 BCE.
